name: Hungary
tagline: From the Magyar conquest of the Carpathian Basin to a European republic, by way of Mohács and Trianon.
summary: Hungary's history begins with the Magyar conquest of the Carpathian Basin around 895-896, when seven nomadic tribes under the chieftain Árpád settled the Danube plain. A century later his great-great-grandson Stephen accepted Christianity and was crowned around the turn of the millennium, founding a Christian kingdom that endured for half a thousand years. That medieval kingdom survived the Mongol catastrophe of 1241 but was shattered at the Battle of Mohács in 1526, after which Hungary was split for nearly two centuries among the Ottomans, the Habsburgs and an autonomous Transylvania. Reunited under Habsburg rule, Hungary rose again in the revolution of 1848-49 and won near-parity in the Austro-Hungarian Compromise of 1867. The First World War brought the Treaty of Trianon of 1920, the defining national trauma, which stripped Hungary of about two-thirds of its territory and population and left some three million ethnic Hungarians beyond the new borders. Drawn into alliance with Nazi Germany, occupied and host to the mass murder of its Jews in 1944, Hungary then spent four decades under communism, rose against Soviet rule in 1956, and finally helped pull down the Iron Curtain in 1989 before joining NATO in 1999 and the European Union in 2004.

founding.label: The Magyar conquest and the crowning of Saint Stephen
founding.detail: Hungary traces its origin to the Honfoglalás, the 'land-taking' of around 895-896, when seven Magyar tribes led by the chieftain Árpád crossed the Carpathian passes and settled the Carpathian Basin, founding the dynasty that would rule for four centuries. The Christian kingdom itself dates to the turn of the millennium: Árpád's descendant Stephen, baptised a Christian, was crowned the first king of Hungary around 1000-1001, traditionally with a crown sent by Pope Sylvester II. Stephen organised his realm on Western models, founded bishoprics and abbeys, and bound Hungary to Latin Christendom, for which he was later canonised as Saint Stephen. The kingdom he founded endured until it was destroyed at the Battle of Mohács in 1526.

quickFacts.label: Magyar conquest
quickFacts.value: Honfoglalás, the settlement of the Carpathian Basin under Árpád, c. 895-896

quickFacts.label: Christian kingdom founded
quickFacts.value: Stephen I crowned first king of Hungary, c. 1000-1001

quickFacts.label: Medieval kingdom destroyed
quickFacts.value: Ottoman victory at the Battle of Mohács, 29 August 1526

quickFacts.label: The Trianon trauma
quickFacts.value: Treaty of Trianon, 4 June 1920: about two-thirds of territory lost

quickFacts.label: European Union
quickFacts.value: Joined NATO in 1999 and the EU on 1 May 2004

eras.title: The Magyar Conquest and the Christian Kingdom
eras.standfirst: Seven nomadic tribes seized the Carpathian Basin, and within a century their leaders turned a steppe confederation into a Christian kingdom of Latin Europe.
body: Around 895-896 a confederation of Magyar tribes, led westward from the steppe by the chieftain Árpád, crossed the north-eastern passes of the Carpathians and took possession of the Carpathian Basin in the event Hungarians remember as the Honfoglalás, the 'land-taking'. Árpád, chosen to lead the seven tribes, gave his name to the dynasty that would rule Hungary until 1301; under it the nation was transformed from a confederation of tribes into a powerful state of east-central Europe. For half a century the Magyars raided deep into western Europe, until their defeat by the German king Otto I at the Lechfeld in 955 turned them toward settlement and statehood.

The decisive turn came at the close of the tenth century. Árpád's descendant Géza opened relations with the Latin West and began the Christianisation of his people; in 996 Benedictine monks were settled at Pannonhalma, founding the first Christian monastery in Hungary, a house that would convert the Magyars, found the country's first school and, in 1055, produce the first surviving document written in Hungarian. Géza's son Stephen completed the work. Crowned the first king of Hungary around 1000-1001, traditionally with a crown sent by Pope Sylvester II, Stephen organised the kingdom on German models, founded bishoprics and abbeys, made church-building and tithing obligatory, and secured Hungary's place among the Christian princes of Europe. He was later canonised as Saint Stephen, and the Holy Crown that bears his name became the sacred symbol of Hungarian statehood.

The kingdom Stephen founded grew into a major medieval power under the Árpád kings, reaching the peak of its authority under Béla III in the later twelfth century. That ascent was nearly ended by catastrophe. In 1241 the Mongols under Batu Khan and the general Subutai swept into Hungary and destroyed the army of King Béla IV at the Battle of Mohi on the Sajó River; the king fled to the Adriatic coast and for a year, in effect, the kingdom of Hungary ceased to exist. The Mongols withdrew in 1242 only because the death of the Great Khan recalled their leaders eastward, and Béla returned to rebuild a devastated realm, fortifying his towns in stone against any future invasion.

events.title: The Magyar conquest (Honfoglalás)
events.summary: Seven Magyar tribes under the chieftain Árpád crossed the Carpathians and seized the Carpathian Basin, founding the Árpád dynasty and the settlement that gave rise to the Hungarian state.

events.title: Pannonhalma Abbey founded
events.summary: Benedictine monks settled at Pannonhalma, founding Hungary's first Christian monastery; it would help convert the Magyars, found the country's first school and, in 1055, produce the first document written in Hungarian.

events.title: Coronation of Stephen I
events.summary: Stephen, of the Árpád line, was crowned the first king of Hungary, traditionally with a crown from Pope Sylvester II, founding a Christian kingdom organised on Western models. He was later canonised as Saint Stephen.

events.title: Mongol invasion and the Battle of Mohi
events.summary: The Mongols under Batu Khan destroyed King Béla IV's army at the Sajó River and ravaged Hungary for a year, leaving much of the kingdom in ruins before withdrawing in 1242.

figures.name: Árpád
figures.blurb: Chosen to lead the seven Hungarian tribes westward, Árpád led the conquest of the Carpathian Basin around 895-896 and founded the Árpád dynasty, which ruled Hungary until 1301.

figures.name: Stephen I (Saint Stephen)
figures.blurb: Crowned around 1000-1001, Stephen converted his realm to Latin Christianity, founded its church and administration and won recognition for Hungary as a Christian kingdom. Canonised in 1083, he remains the country's patron and founding saint.

pullquote.text: Under the Árpád dynasty the Hungarian nation was transformed from a confederation of tribes into a powerful state of east-central Europe.
pullquote.attribution: On the founding dynasty of Hungary

eras.title: Mohács and the Three-Part Kingdom
eras.standfirst: A catastrophic defeat by the Ottomans in 1526 destroyed the medieval kingdom and split Hungary three ways for nearly two centuries.
body: When the Árpád line died out in 1301, the crown passed to foreign dynasties, among them the Angevins and, in the fifteenth century, Matthias Corvinus, under whose Renaissance court Hungary flourished as one of the leading powers of central Europe. But beyond the southern frontier the Ottoman Empire was advancing, and the kingdom's strength was sapped by noble factionalism and a great peasant revolt in 1514. The reckoning came on 29 August 1526 at the Battle of Mohács, where Sultan Süleyman the Magnificent annihilated the army of the young King Louis II. Louis himself died in flight, and with him the medieval Hungarian kingdom effectively ended.

Mohács opened Hungary to a century and a half of division. The country fractured into three parts: a 'Royal Hungary' in the north and west under the Habsburgs, who claimed the crown; a central 'Ottoman Hungary' ruled directly from Buda, which the Turks took in 1541; and an autonomous Principality of Transylvania in the east, a vassal of the sultan that nonetheless preserved a measure of Hungarian self-government and, in the Edict of Torda of 1568, an unusual degree of religious toleration. For ordinary Hungarians the era meant near-constant frontier warfare, depopulation and the burden of a divided homeland.

The tide turned only late in the seventeenth century. After the failed Ottoman siege of Vienna in 1683, a Habsburg-led Christian coalition pushed the Turks back across Hungary, retaking Buda in 1686. The reconquest was confirmed by the Treaty of Karlowitz in 1699, by which the Ottomans ceded almost all of Hungary to the Habsburgs. Liberation from the Turks, however, meant subjection to Vienna: Hungary was reunited, but as a kingdom firmly within the Habsburg monarchy rather than as an independent state.

events.title: Siege of Belgrade
events.summary: Hungarian forces under John Hunyadi broke an Ottoman siege of Belgrade, checking the Turkish advance into central Europe for decades and becoming a celebrated victory of the medieval kingdom.

events.title: Battle of Mohács
events.summary: Süleyman the Magnificent destroyed the Hungarian army and killed King Louis II, ending the medieval kingdom and opening Hungary to Ottoman and Habsburg domination.

events.title: Ottoman capture of Buda and the three-part division
events.summary: The Ottomans seized Buda and Hungary fractured into Royal Hungary under the Habsburgs, Ottoman Hungary ruled from Buda, and the autonomous Principality of Transylvania.

events.title: Reconquest of Buda
events.summary: A Habsburg-led Christian coalition retook Buda from the Ottomans, part of the campaign that drove the Turks from Hungary after more than a century.

events.title: Treaty of Karlowitz
events.summary: The Ottomans ceded almost all of Hungary to the Habsburgs, reuniting the country but binding it firmly within the Habsburg monarchy.

figures.name: Louis II
figures.blurb: King of Hungary and Bohemia, Louis led the Christian army at Mohács and died in flight after its destruction at the age of twenty, his death marking the collapse of the independent medieval kingdom.

figures.name: Süleyman the Magnificent
figures.blurb: The Ottoman sultan whose victory at Mohács in 1526 broke Hungary; his armies took Buda in 1541 and made central Hungary a province of the empire for nearly a century and a half.

pullquote.text: The defeat at Mohács destroyed the unified Hungarian nation and opened the country to both Habsburg and Ottoman domination.

eras.title: Habsburg Rule, Revolution and the Dual Monarchy
eras.standfirst: Under the Habsburgs, Hungary's national awakening exploded in the revolution of 1848 and, after its defeat, won near-equality in the Compromise of 1867.
body: Eighteenth-century Hungary was a kingdom of the Habsburg monarchy, repopulated after the Turkish wars and ruled from Vienna, though the Hungarian nobility clung jealously to their ancient liberties. The early nineteenth century brought a vigorous national 'Reform Era', led by figures such as Count István Széchenyi and the radical Lajos Kossuth, who pressed for the modernisation of the economy, the spread of the Hungarian language and the loosening of Habsburg control. These currents converged in the European revolutions of 1848.

On 15 March 1848, inspired by revolution in Paris and Vienna and led by intellectuals including the poet Sándor Petőfi, a bloodless rising in Pest won sweeping reforms: the abolition of censorship and serfdom, general taxation and a responsible Hungarian government embodied in the March Laws. When Vienna moved to reverse these gains, the conflict turned to war. In April 1849 Hungary, with Kossuth as its leader, proclaimed full independence. The revolution was crushed only when the Austrian emperor called in a Russian army; the Hungarian forces surrendered in August 1849, and a period of harsh repression followed, with executions of rebel officers and Kossuth driven into lifelong exile.

Defeat abroad eventually forced compromise. Weakened by its defeat in the war with Prussia in 1866, the Habsburg monarchy came to terms with Hungarian demands. The Austro-Hungarian Compromise of 1867, the Ausgleich, transformed the empire into the Dual Monarchy of Austria-Hungary: Emperor Francis Joseph granted Hungary full internal autonomy with its own parliament and government, while foreign affairs, defence and their financing remained common to both halves of the realm. The half-century that followed was, for Hungary, an age of rapid growth, with Budapest blossoming into a great metropolis whose banks of the Danube, Buda Castle quarter and grand Andrássy Avenue, the latter built above continental Europe's first underground railway, would later be recognised as a UNESCO World Heritage site.

events.title: The Revolution of 1848
events.summary: A bloodless rising in Pest, with Sándor Petőfi among its voices, won the March Laws abolishing censorship and serfdom and establishing a responsible Hungarian government against Habsburg absolutism.

events.title: Defeat of the war of independence
events.summary: After Hungary proclaimed independence under Kossuth, the revolution was crushed by a combined Austrian and Russian army; the surrender brought executions and Kossuth's exile.

events.title: Austro-Hungarian Compromise (Ausgleich)
events.summary: The Ausgleich transformed the empire into the Dual Monarchy, granting Hungary full internal autonomy with its own parliament while keeping defence and foreign affairs common to Austria-Hungary.

events.title: The founding of Budapest
events.summary: Buda, Óbuda and Pest were unified into a single capital that grew into one of Europe's great cities; its historic core along the Danube is today a UNESCO World Heritage site.

figures.name: Lajos Kossuth
figures.blurb: Journalist and orator who became the driving force of the 1848 revolution and, in 1849, leader of an independent Hungary. After its defeat he spent the rest of his long life in exile, denouncing Habsburg rule across Europe and America.

figures.name: Sándor Petőfi
figures.blurb: Hungary's national poet, whose verse gave voice to the rising of 15 March 1848. He vanished in battle in 1849, becoming an enduring symbol of the revolution and of Hungarian liberty.

pullquote.text: The Ausgleich of 1867 gave Hungary full internal autonomy and its own parliament, transforming the empire into the Dual Monarchy of Austria-Hungary.

eras.title: Trianon and the Shrinking of Hungary
eras.standfirst: Defeat in the First World War brought the Treaty of Trianon, which stripped Hungary of two-thirds of its land and left millions of Magyars beyond the new borders.
body: Hungary entered the First World War as a partner in Austria-Hungary, and shared in the empire's collapse in 1918. As the monarchy disintegrated, a short-lived democratic republic gave way in 1919 to a communist regime under Béla Kun and then, after its overthrow, to the conservative-nationalist order of Regent Miklós Horthy, who ruled a 'kingdom without a king' from 1920. The new Hungary's borders were dictated by the victorious Allies.

On 4 June 1920, in the Trianon Palace at Versailles, Hungary signed the treaty that became the defining trauma of its modern history. By the Treaty of Trianon, Hungary was shorn of about two-thirds of its former territory and roughly two-thirds of its inhabitants. Transylvania passed to Romania; Slovakia and Ruthenia to the new Czechoslovakia; Croatia and the Vojvodina to the new Yugoslav state; and lesser districts to Austria, Poland and Italy. Around three million ethnic Hungarians were left living as minorities outside the borders of the truncated state, and the army was capped at 35,000 men.

The sense of mutilation defined interwar Hungary. Revision of Trianon became the central national cause, the slogan 'Nem, nem, soha' ('No, no, never') a popular refrain. The pursuit of the lost lands drew Hungary toward the revisionist powers, Italy and Nazi Germany, which rewarded it with the Vienna Awards of 1938 and 1940, restoring parts of Slovakia and northern Transylvania. These gains, made in Hitler's shadow, bound Hungary ever more tightly to Germany and set the country on the road to the Second World War.

events.title: Collapse of Austria-Hungary
events.summary: Defeat in the First World War destroyed the Dual Monarchy; amid revolution Hungary passed through a democratic republic and a brief communist regime before the conservative order of Regent Horthy.

events.title: The Treaty of Trianon
events.summary: Hungary lost about two-thirds of its territory and population, leaving roughly three million ethnic Hungarians outside the new borders in Romania, Czechoslovakia, Yugoslavia and elsewhere; the trauma shaped Hungarian politics for a century.

events.title: First Vienna Award
events.summary: Nazi Germany and Fascist Italy awarded Hungary parts of southern Slovakia from Czechoslovakia, the first of the revisionist gains that drew Hungary toward the Axis.

events.title: Second Vienna Award
events.summary: Germany and Italy awarded Hungary northern Transylvania from Romania, deepening Hungary's dependence on the Axis powers in its quest to reverse Trianon.

figures.name: Miklós Horthy
figures.blurb: Former admiral who became regent in 1920 and ruled interwar Hungary as a 'kingdom without a king'. His pursuit of treaty revision allied Hungary with Nazi Germany, a course that ended in occupation and catastrophe.

figures.name: Count István Bethlen
figures.blurb: Dominant statesman of the 1920s who stabilised Hungary after the upheavals of 1918-20 while making the revision of Trianon the guiding aim of national policy.

pullquote.text: By the terms of the Treaty of Trianon, Hungary was shorn of at least two-thirds of its former territory and about two-thirds of its inhabitants.

eras.title: The Second World War and the Holocaust in Hungary
eras.standfirst: Allied to Germany in pursuit of the lost lands, Hungary was drawn into war, occupied in 1944, and made the scene of the mass murder of its Jews.
body: Hungary entered the Second World War as an ally of Nazi Germany, joining the invasion of the Soviet Union in 1941 in the hope of securing the territories restored by the Vienna Awards. As the war turned against the Axis, the regent Horthy sought secretly to extract Hungary from the alliance. Germany pre-empted him: on 19 March 1944 German forces occupied Hungary and installed a compliant government, even as the country still held the largest surviving Jewish community in Europe, some 760,000 to 780,000 people.

What followed was among the swiftest and most concentrated episodes of the Holocaust. Between 15 May and 9 July 1944, with Hungarian gendarmes and officials carrying out the round-ups under German direction, approximately 437,000 Jews were deported from Hungary on 147 trains, the great majority to Auschwitz-Birkenau, where roughly three-quarters were murdered in the gas chambers on arrival. On 7 July 1944 Horthy ordered the deportations halted, but the killing resumed under the fascist Arrow Cross regime installed by Germany in October 1944, which murdered thousands more Jews in Budapest. In all, around half a million Hungarian Jews perished.

The war devastated Hungary itself. The country became a battlefield as the Red Army advanced; the siege of Budapest over the winter of 1944-45 reduced much of the historic city to rubble, with both Buda Castle and the bridges across the Danube destroyed. By the spring of 1945 the German and Hungarian forces had been driven out, the territorial gains of the Vienna Awards lost again, and Hungary lay open to occupation by the Soviet Union.

events.title: Hungary enters the war as an Axis ally
events.summary: Seeking to keep and extend the lands restored by the Vienna Awards, Hungary joined Germany's war and took part in the invasion of the Soviet Union.

events.title: German occupation of Hungary
events.summary: German forces occupied Hungary to keep it in the war and to deport its Jews, then the largest surviving Jewish community in Europe, numbering some 760,000 to 780,000 people.

events.title: Deportation of the Hungarian Jews
events.summary: Between 15 May and 9 July 1944 about 437,000 Jews were deported, mostly to Auschwitz-Birkenau, where some three-quarters were murdered on arrival; Horthy halted the trains on 7 July.

events.title: The Arrow Cross seizure of power
events.summary: After Horthy's attempt to leave the war, Germany installed the fascist Arrow Cross, which murdered thousands more Jews in Budapest before the Soviet conquest of the city.

events.title: The siege of Budapest
events.summary: The months-long battle for the capital between German-Hungarian and Soviet forces destroyed much of historic Budapest, including the castle and the Danube bridges, as the Red Army completed its conquest of Hungary.

figures.name: Raoul Wallenberg
figures.blurb: A Swedish diplomat in Budapest who issued protective passes and sheltered tens of thousands of Jews during the Arrow Cross terror of 1944. Detained by Soviet forces in 1945, he vanished into the Soviet prison system.

figures.name: Ferenc Szálasi
figures.blurb: Head of the fascist Arrow Cross movement installed in power by Germany in October 1944. His regime murdered thousands of Budapest's Jews; he was tried and executed for war crimes after the war.

pullquote.text: In under two months in 1944, roughly 437,000 Jews were deported from Hungary, the great majority to be murdered at Auschwitz-Birkenau.

eras.title: Communism, 1956 and the Return to Europe
eras.standfirst: Four decades of communist rule, defied in the 1956 revolution and softened by 'goulash communism', ended in 1989 when Hungary helped open the Iron Curtain.
body: Liberation from Germany brought subjection to the Soviet Union. By 1949 the communists, backed by Moscow, had seized total power and proclaimed the Hungarian People's Republic, a one-party state under the Stalinist Mátyás Rákosi marked by terror, show trials and forced collectivisation. Discontent erupted in October 1956. Beginning with a student demonstration in Budapest on 23 October, a nationwide revolution swept the country; the reform communist Imre Nagy became premier, formed a multi-party government and declared Hungary's withdrawal from the Warsaw Pact. The Soviet response was overwhelming: at dawn on 4 November 1956 the Red Army attacked, crushing the rising within days. Some 2,500 Hungarians were killed, around 200,000 fled abroad as refugees, and Nagy was seized and, two years later, executed.

Soviet power was restored under János Kádár, installed in 1956, who ruled until 1988. After an initial wave of reprisals, Kádár softened the regime under the maxim 'he who is not against us is with us'. His New Economic Mechanism of 1968 admitted limited market forces and private enterprise, producing a relative prosperity and personal latitude unusual in the Soviet bloc, the system nicknamed 'goulash communism'. Hungary remained a one-party state loyal to Moscow, but daily life was freer and more comfortable than elsewhere behind the Iron Curtain.

In 1989 Hungary helped bring the whole communist order down. On 19 August 1989, at the Pan-European Picnic on the Austro-Hungarian border near Sopron, a symbolic gate was opened and some 700 East Germans fled west; weeks later Hungary opened its border fully, breaching the Iron Curtain and setting off the chain reaction that toppled the Berlin Wall. On 23 October 1989, the anniversary of the 1956 rising, Hungary proclaimed itself a free republic. The country built a democracy and a market economy, joined NATO on 12 March 1999 and acceded to the European Union on 1 May 2004 in its largest-ever enlargement, completing Hungary's return to the West. The fate of the roughly three million ethnic Hungarians left beyond the borders by Trianon, in Romania, Slovakia, Serbia and Ukraine, has remained a defining theme of the country's relations with its neighbours.

events.title: The Hungarian People's Republic
events.summary: Backed by the Soviet Union, the communists seized total power and proclaimed a one-party People's Republic, imposing Stalinist terror, show trials and forced collectivisation under Mátyás Rákosi.

events.title: The Hungarian Revolution of 1956
events.summary: A nationwide rising under premier Imre Nagy declared Hungary's withdrawal from the Warsaw Pact; the Soviet army crushed it on 4 November, killing some 2,500 Hungarians and driving about 200,000 into exile.

events.title: 'Goulash communism' and the New Economic Mechanism
events.summary: János Kádár's reforms admitted limited market forces and private enterprise, giving Hungarians a relative prosperity and freedom unusual in the Soviet bloc.

events.title: The Pan-European Picnic and the opening of the border
events.summary: A border gate opened near Sopron let some 700 East Germans flee west; Hungary's subsequent full opening of its frontier breached the Iron Curtain and helped bring down the Berlin Wall.

events.title: Proclamation of the republic
events.summary: On the anniversary of the 1956 rising Hungary proclaimed itself a free republic, ending four decades of communist rule and beginning its transition to democracy.

events.title: Accession to NATO and the European Union
events.summary: Having joined NATO on 12 March 1999, Hungary acceded to the European Union on 1 May 2004 in the bloc's largest single enlargement, completing its return to the West.

figures.name: Imre Nagy
figures.blurb: Premier during the 1956 revolution, Nagy backed a multi-party government and declared Hungary's neutrality and exit from the Warsaw Pact. After the Soviet crushing of the rising he was captured and, in 1958, executed; he was reburied with honours in 1989.

figures.name: János Kádár
figures.blurb: Installed by the Soviets in 1956 and leader until 1988, Kádár presided over the reprisals after the revolution but then built the relatively liberal 'goulash communism' that made Hungary the most relaxed state in the Soviet bloc.

pullquote.text: The opening of the border near Sopron in August 1989 was a link in the chain reaction that brought down the Iron Curtain and ended the division of Europe.

figures.name: Árpád
figures.blurb: Chieftain who led the Magyar tribes into the Carpathian Basin around 895-896 and founded the dynasty that ruled Hungary until 1301, giving the new nation its first leadership.

figures.name: Stephen I (Saint Stephen)
figures.blurb: Crowned around 1000-1001, Stephen founded the Christian kingdom of Hungary, organised its church and state on Western models and was canonised as the country's patron saint.

figures.name: Lajos Kossuth
figures.blurb: Voice of Hungary's national awakening and leader of the war of independence in 1849, who spent his last decades in exile championing the Hungarian cause across the world.

figures.name: Imre Nagy
figures.blurb: Reform communist who led the 1956 revolution and proclaimed Hungary's neutrality before the Soviet army crushed the rising; executed in 1958, he became a martyr of Hungarian freedom.

sources.label: Millenary Benedictine Abbey of Pannonhalma and its Natural Environment
The Holy Crown of Hungary and the Coronation Regalia
Budapest, including the Banks of the Danube, the Buda Castle Quarter and Andrássy Avenue
EU enlargement: Hungary joined on 1 May 2004
